guidelineReferences.name: 2019 ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism
guidelineReferences.citation: Konstantinides SV, et al. Eur Heart J. 2020;41:543-603
keyPoints: High-risk PE presents with hemodynamic instability - shock or sustained hypotension
Wells score and Geneva score help assess clinical probability
D-dimer has high NPV in low-probability patients
CTPA is the diagnostic standard for PE
Massive PE may require thrombolysis or embolectomy
RV dysfunction on echo indicates intermediate-high risk

female-28-pe.name: K. Williams
female-28-pe.chiefComplaint: I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing
female-28-pe.variantDescription: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Looks like anxiety, actually PE

missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Patient diagnosed with 'panic attack' and sent home with lorazepam (Ativan). Collapses at home 4 hours later from massive PE extending into bilateral pulmonary arteries. Dies in ambulance en route to hospital.
missedConsequences.Anxiety/Panic Attack: If this were actually anxiety (it's not), sending home would have been appropriate. This case demonstrates why anxiety must be a diagnosis of EXCLUSION.

commonPitfalls: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the 'obvious' diagnosis (anxiety) is WRONG
Young, healthy-appearing woman with 'anxiety symptoms' - easy to dismiss as psychiatric
Missing the subtle hypoxia (SpO2 94%) - anxiety doesn't cause hypoxia!
Not asking about OCP use - estrogen is a major VTE risk factor
Dismissing leg swelling as 'normal' or from long car ride
Forgetting that PE can present with anxiety-like symptoms - sense of doom is classic
Being reassured by 'normal' chest X-ray - CXR is often normal in PE
CRITICAL: Anxiety is a diagnosis of EXCLUSION - must rule out organic causes first

mcqs.question: The patient is a young woman on oral contraceptives who just returned from a long flight. She feels anxious and short of breath. What's the MOST important next consideration?

options.text: She probably has panic disorder - prescribe lorazepam
options.explanation: DANGEROUS! Never diagnose anxiety without ruling out organic causes. OCPs + immobility = major PE risk.

options.text: She's hyperventilating from anxiety - have her breathe into a paper bag
options.explanation: Paper bag breathing is contraindicated if there's any possibility of hypoxia from organic cause.

options.text: Recognize multiple PE risk factors (OCPs, immobility) and maintain high suspicion for VTE
options.explanation: OCPs increase VTE risk 3-4x. Long flights add immobility risk. Combined with dyspnea and tachycardia = must rule out PE.

options.text: This is classic panic attack presentation - reassure and observe
options.explanation: Classic error. These same symptoms occur in PE. Risk factors demand workup.

mcqs.question: Vital signs show SpO2 of 94% on room air. The patient says she feels like she's dying. In a 'panic attack,' what would you expect the SpO2 to be?

options.text: Low (around 94%) - panic attacks cause hypoxia from hyperventilation
options.explanation: WRONG! Hyperventilation actually INCREASES oxygen levels. Panic attacks typically have SpO2 of 99-100%.

options.text: Normal to high (99-100%) - hyperventilation increases oxygenation
options.explanation: Hyperventilation blows off CO2 and increases O2. SpO2 of 94% in a 'panic attack' should trigger immediate concern for PE, pneumonia, or other organic cause.

options.text: Variable - SpO2 is not reliable in anxiety
options.explanation: SpO2 is very reliable. Low SpO2 should never be attributed to anxiety.

options.text: SpO2 doesn't matter in psychiatric conditions
options.explanation: SpO2 matters enormously - it helps distinguish organic from psychiatric causes.

mcqs.question: You're considering whether to order a CT-PA. The chest X-ray is normal. Does a normal CXR rule out PE?

options.text: Yes - if CXR is normal, PE is unlikely
options.explanation: WRONG! CXR is often normal in PE. Up to 40% of PE patients have normal or near-normal CXR.

options.text: No - CXR is often normal in PE and should not provide false reassurance
options.explanation: Classic PE CXR findings (Hampton's hump, Westermark sign) are often absent. A normal CXR with hypoxia actually INCREASES PE suspicion.

options.text: CXR can definitively diagnose PE if you know what to look for
options.explanation: CXR cannot definitively diagnose PE. CT-PA or V/Q scan is required.

options.text: You should get a V/Q scan instead of CXR
options.explanation: V/Q is used when CT-PA is contraindicated. CXR is appropriate initial imaging but doesn't rule out PE.

allDiagnoses.name: Anxiety/Panic Attack
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young woman with acute dyspnea, palpitations, sense of doom - classic panic attack presentation. BUT this is a TRAP. These symptoms also occur in PE. Must rule out organic causes first!

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperventilation Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Tachypnea and feeling of breathlessness can be from hyperventilation. BUT hyperventilation doesn't cause hypoxia - SpO2 should be HIGH not low.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ THE ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Risk factors: OCPs, recent long flight (immobility). Symptoms: dyspnea, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, leg swelling. SpO2 94% is a RED FLAG - anxiety doesn't cause hypoxia!

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Coronary Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Unusual in 28-year-old woman but OCPs do increase cardiovascular risk. Must consider.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumothorax
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause acute dyspnea and chest pain. Would expect decreased breath sounds on affected side.

allDiagnoses.name: Arrhythmia
allDiagnoses.rationale: SVT, AFib could cause palpitations and dyspnea. ECG essential.

allDiagnoses.name: Asthma Exacerbation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could cause dyspnea but no history of asthma, no wheezing, no known triggers.

allDiagnoses.name: Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Severe anemia can cause dyspnea and tachycardia. Would not expect acute presentation without bleeding.

allDiagnoses.name: Thyrotoxicosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause anxiety-like symptoms and tachycardia. Usually more chronic presentation.

female-28-pe.oldcartsNarrative: "I don't know what's happening to me. I suddenly started feeling like I couldn't get enough air about an hour ago. My heart is pounding out of my chest. I feel like something terrible is going to happen - like I'm going to die. I've had panic attacks before but this feels different somehow, worse. I have some chest tightness, kind of on my right side, and it hurts a little when I take a deep breath. My left leg has been a little swollen since I got back from visiting my parents - I drove 12 hours each way last week. I'm on birth control pills. I don't smoke. I'm generally healthy. I've just been so stressed lately with work. My friends keep telling me I need to relax."

historyQuestions.text: When exactly did the shortness of breath start?
historyQuestions.answer: About an hour ago. I was just sitting at my desk at work and suddenly felt like I couldn't catch my breath. It came on pretty suddenly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset dyspnea at rest - concerning for PE. Panic attacks can also be acute, but PE must be ruled out first.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the chest discomfort - is it sharp, dull, pressure?
historyQuestions.answer: It's kind of sharp, on my right side. It gets worse when I take a deep breath.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PLEURITIC CHEST PAIN (sharp, worse with inspiration) is classic for PE. Pulmonary infarction irritates the pleura.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned your leg is swollen - tell me more about that.
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, my left calf has been bigger than my right since I got back from that long drive. It's a little achy too. I figured it was just from sitting so long.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 🚨 RED FLAG: Unilateral leg swelling after prolonged immobility = DVT until proven otherwise. DVT is the source of most PEs.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your recent travel.
historyQuestions.answer: I drove to visit my parents last week - 12 hours each way. I only stopped a couple times for gas. My leg started swelling on the drive back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged immobility (>4 hours) is a major VTE risk factor. Combined with OCPs = very high risk.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. I've been on them for about 3 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OCPs increase VTE risk 3-4 fold. Estrogen promotes clot formation. This is a major risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned having panic attacks before - how does this compare?
historyQuestions.answer: I've had maybe 3-4 panic attacks over the years. This feels similar in some ways - the racing heart, feeling scared. But those went away after like 20 minutes. This has been an hour and it's not getting better. And I've never had the chest pain or leg thing before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: KEY DISTINCTION: Patient recognizes this feels different. New symptoms (chest pain, leg swelling) and prolonged duration suggest organic cause.

historyQuestions.text: Have you coughed up any blood?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I haven't had any cough really.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hemoptysis can occur with PE (pulmonary infarction) but is not always present.

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt like you might pass out?
historyQuestions.answer: A little lightheaded, yeah. I haven't actually fainted.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Near-syncope with PE suggests hemodynamic compromise - concerning for large clot burden.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of blood clots?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually, my mom had a blood clot in her leg when she was pregnant with my brother.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of VTE suggests possible inherited thrombophilia. Greatly increases pretest probability of PE.

occupation_current_activity_level.answer: I work from home now. I sit at my computer most of the day. Don't get up much.
occupation_current_activity_level.relevance: Prolonged immobility is major VTE risk factor. Work-from-home with sedentary job significantly increases clot risk.

social_recent_travel.answer: I flew to visit my parents last month. Long flight, about 5 hours.
social_recent_travel.relevance: Air travel is recognized VTE risk factor, especially combined with OCPs and sedentary lifestyle.

social_contraception.answer: I've been on the pill for about 3 years now.
social_contraception.relevance: Oral contraceptives significantly increase VTE risk, especially combined with family history and immobility.

social_lifestyle_immobility.answer: I don't exercise much. During busy work weeks I barely leave my apartment.
social_lifestyle_immobility.relevance: Sedentary lifestyle with prolonged sitting compounds VTE risk from other factors.

social_stress_work.answer: Work has been really stressful. Long hours at the computer, tight deadlines.
social_stress_work.relevance: Work stress noted but key issue is prolonged sitting, not stress itself.

housing_work_setup.answer: My desk is in my bedroom. I don't have a good ergonomic setup. I sit for hours.
housing_work_setup.relevance: Poor work-from-home ergonomics leads to prolonged sitting - major VTE risk factor.

diet_hydration.answer: I forget to drink water when I'm working. Probably not enough.
diet_hydration.relevance: Dehydration increases blood viscosity and VTE risk.

social_smoking.relevance: Non-smoker - one less VTE risk factor, but multiple others present.

physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 SpO2 94% is the critical finding. ANXIETY DOES NOT CAUSE HYPOXIA. Tachycardia and tachypnea support PE.

physicalExam.finding: Young woman appearing anxious, sitting upright, mild respiratory distress. Not diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Appearance is consistent with either anxiety or PE - cannot distinguish based on appearance alone.

physicalExam.finding: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lungs are often CLEAR in PE! This should NOT be reassuring. PE doesn't cause abnormal lung sounds.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs. Possible loud P2 (hard to assess with tachycardia).
physicalExam.reasoning: Loud P2 suggests pulmonary hypertension from PE. Regular rhythm argues against AFib as cause.

physicalExam.finding: JVP mildly elevated at approximately 10 cm H2O.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated JVP indicates right heart strain - consistent with significant PE causing pulmonary hypertension.

physicalExam.finding: LEFT calf circumference 38 cm, RIGHT calf 34 cm (>2 cm difference). Left calf is warm, tender to palpation, with trace pitting edema. Positive Homan's sign (pain with dorsiflexion).
physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 ASYMMETRIC LEG SWELLING WITH TENDERNESS = DVT. This is the smoking gun. Homan's sign has poor sensitivity but when present is concerning.

physicalExam.finding: No chest wall tenderness. No reproducible pain with palpation.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-reproducible pain argues against musculoskeletal cause. Pleuritic pain from PE is not reproducible with palpation.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: D-dimer elevated 5x normal. While D-dimer can be elevated in many conditions, combined with high pretest probability, this strongly supports PE.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Elevated troponin in PE indicates right heart strain and is a poor prognostic sign. Suggests significant clot burden.

labs.name: BNP
labs.reasoning: Elevated BNP supports right heart strain from PE.

labs.name: ABG
labs.result: pH 7.48, pCO2 28, pO2 68, A-a gradient elevated at 42 (normal <15 for her age)
labs.reasoning: Respiratory alkalosis (low pCO2 from hyperventilation) with hypoxemia and widened A-a gradient - classic ABG pattern for PE.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Normal CBC, no evidence of infection or anemia.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Clear lungs. No infiltrate, no effusion, no pneumothorax. Heart size normal. Subtle decreased vascular markings in right lung field (Westermark sign).
imaging.reasoning: CXR often normal in PE! Westermark sign (oligemia) is present but subtle. Normal CXR should NOT rule out PE.

imaging.name: CT Pulmonary Angiography
imaging.result: POSITIVE: Large saddle embolus at main pulmonary artery bifurcation with extension into bilateral main pulmonary arteries. Right heart strain with RV:LV ratio 1.3 (normal <1.0). Multiple bilateral segmental PEs.
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: Massive saddle PE with right heart strain. This is life-threatening and requires immediate intervention.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: Right ventricle dilated with decreased function. McConnell's sign present (RV free wall akinesis with preserved apex). RVSP estimated at 55 mmHg (elevated).
imaging.reasoning: Echo confirms right heart strain. McConnell's sign is highly specific for acute PE. Elevated RVSP indicates pulmonary hypertension from clot.

imaging.name: Lower extremity venous duplex
imaging.result: Non-compressible left common femoral vein and left popliteal vein. Acute DVT confirmed.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms DVT as source of PE. Important for treatment planning and assessing extent of clot burden.

treatments.name: Start anticoagulation (heparin)
treatments.result: Heparin bolus 80 units/kg followed by 18 units/kg/hr infusion started.
treatments.reasoning: Anticoagulation is first-line treatment for PE. Heparin prevents clot propagation. Do not wait for imaging if suspicion is high.

treatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
treatments.result: Nasal cannula 2L applied, SpO2 improved to 97%.
treatments.reasoning: Correct hypoxemia. Patient is hypoxic and needs supplemental O2.

treatments.name: Consider thrombolysis (tPA)
treatments.result: Given hemodynamic stability, decision made to anticoagulate and monitor. tPA held in reserve if patient decompensates.
treatments.reasoning: tPA considered for massive PE with hemodynamic instability. This patient is borderline - close monitoring essential.

treatments.name: Benzodiazepine for anxiety
treatments.result: HOLD - treating underlying cause (PE) first. Benzos could mask deterioration.
treatments.reasoning: DO NOT give sedatives until organic cause ruled out. Benzos could mask respiratory decompensation.

treatments.name: IV fluids cautiously
treatments.reasoning: Careful with fluids in PE - RV is already strained. Aggressive fluids can worsen RV failure.

consults.name: Pulmonology/Critical Care consult
consults.result: Pulm/CC consulted for massive PE management.
consults.reasoning: Massive PE requires specialist involvement for decisions about thrombolysis, catheter-directed therapy, or ECMO.

consults.name: Interventional Radiology consult
consults.result: IR aware, available for catheter-directed thrombolysis if needed.
consults.reasoning: IR can perform catheter-directed thrombolysis or thrombectomy if patient deteriorates.

consults.name: Psychiatry consult for anxiety
consults.result: NOT NEEDED - this is not anxiety, it's PE
consults.reasoning: Patient does NOT have anxiety - she has PE. Psych consult would delay life-saving treatment.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admitted to ICU for close monitoring given massive PE with RV strain.
disposition.reasoning: Massive PE with RV strain requires ICU-level monitoring. Risk of deterioration is high.

disposition.name: Discharge with anxiolytic and follow-up
disposition.result: ABSOLUTELY NOT - this would be fatal
disposition.reasoning: Patient has massive PE. Discharge would result in death. This is the trap - do not fall for it.

actualDiagnosis.name: Massive Pulmonary Embolism (with DVT)
keyFindings: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Presentation mimics anxiety but is life-threatening PE
SpO2 94% - ANXIETY DOES NOT CAUSE HYPOXIA. This is the critical finding that should trigger concern
Risk factors: OCPs + prolonged immobility (12-hour drive) + family history of VTE
Unilateral leg swelling with tenderness - DVT confirmed as source
Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse with inspiration)
CT-PA: Saddle embolus with RV strain - massive PE
Patient recognized 'this feels different' from prior panic attacks - LISTEN to patients
teachingPoints: 🚨 ANXIETY IS A DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION: Never diagnose anxiety without ruling out organic causes, especially with abnormal vital signs.
SpO2 IS YOUR FRIEND: Hyperventilation from anxiety INCREASES oxygen (SpO2 99-100%). Low SpO2 should trigger immediate concern for organic pathology.
WELLS CRITERIA FOR PE: Calculate pretest probability. This patient has high probability (immobility, OCPs, leg swelling, tachycardia, hemoptysis).
OCP + IMMOBILITY = HIGH VTE RISK: These risk factors are synergistic. Always ask about both in young women with dyspnea.
LISTEN TO THE PATIENT: 'This feels different' is valuable information. Patients know their bodies.
CXR DOES NOT RULE OUT PE: CXR is often normal or near-normal in PE. A 'normal' CXR with hypoxia should INCREASE your suspicion.
SENSE OF DOOM: While classic for panic attacks, sense of impending doom is also classic for PE and MI. Don't dismiss it.
SADDLE PE = EMERGENCY: Clot at pulmonary artery bifurcation indicates massive clot burden. High mortality without treatment.

guidelineReferences.name: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: Konstantinides SV et al. Eur Heart J. 2020;41(4):543-603
keyPoints: High-risk PE (hypotensive): Systemic thrombolysis recommended
Intermediate-high risk: Anticoagulation + close monitoring for deterioration
Risk stratification: PESI/sPESI + RV function + biomarkers
Catheter-directed or surgical embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated
DOACs preferred for most stable PE patients

guidelineReferences.name: ACCP Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE 2021
keyPoints: Wells criteria + D-dimer for risk stratification
CTPA is gold standard imaging for PE diagnosis
DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
Treatment duration: 3 months minimum; extended if unprovoked
IVC filters reserved for patients who cannot be anticoagulated

wellsPE.name: Wells Criteria for Pulmonary Embolism
wellsPE.description: Estimates pretest probability of PE to guide diagnostic testing

components.criterion: Clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (leg swelling, tenderness)
PE is #1 diagnosis or equally likely
Immobilization ≥3 days OR surgery in past 4 weeks

thisPatient.clinicalAction: Wells >6 = high probability (~50% have PE). Proceed directly to CTPA. D-dimer not useful at this pretest probability.

low.action: If PERC negative, no testing needed. Otherwise, D-dimer; if negative, PE excluded.

wellsPE.source: Wells PS et al. Thromb Haemost. 2000;83(3):416-420

perc.name: PERC Rule (Pulmonary Embolism Rule-out Criteria)
perc.description: Rules out PE without D-dimer in LOW pretest probability patients

thisPatient.interpretation: Cannot rule out PE - testing required
thisPatient.clinicalAction: Patient fails PERC (tachycardia, hypoxia, leg swelling, OCPs). Must pursue workup.

perc.usage: ONLY use in LOW pretest probability patients. If ALL 8 criteria are ABSENT, PE is effectively ruled out (<2% miss rate) without D-dimer.
perc.source: Kline JA et al. J Thromb Haemost. 2004;2(8):1247-1255

pesi.name: sPESI (Simplified Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index)
pesi.description: Risk stratifies CONFIRMED PE for mortality and adverse outcomes
pesi.note: Use AFTER PE is diagnosed to guide disposition. Score 0 = low risk (outpatient treatment may be appropriate). Score ≥1 = higher risk (consider admission).
pesi.source: Jiménez D et al. Arch Intern Med. 2010;170(15):1383-1389

closureScript.diagnosis: I know you came in thinking this was a panic attack, and I understand why - the symptoms can feel very similar. But we've discovered something more serious. You have blood clots in your lungs, called a pulmonary embolism. The clot started in your leg - that's why your calf was swollen - and traveled to your lungs. Your birth control pills and the long car ride made you more prone to this.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you to the ICU so we can monitor you closely. We've already started blood thinners to stop the clot from growing. The specialist team is deciding if you need any additional treatments. You'll be on blood thinners for several months, and we'll need to discuss your birth control options since estrogen increases clot risk.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Tell us immediately if you feel more short of breath, have any chest pain, feel lightheaded, or cough up any blood. These could mean we need to act more aggressively.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is scary and very different from what you expected. The important thing is that you came in and we caught this. You were right that something felt 'different' from your usual panic attacks - trust that instinct in the future. With treatment, most people recover fully from PE.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the SpO2 was 99% and D-dimer was negative?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but with normal oxygen and negative D-dimer

chiefComplaint.original: I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing
chiefComplaint.modified: Same symptoms but SpO2 99%, D-dimer negative, no leg swelling, no risk factors

mustNotMiss: Still consider PE if high clinical suspicion

feedback.correct: Normal SpO2 + negative D-dimer + low Wells score = PE effectively ruled out. NOW anxiety becomes reasonable diagnosis. Key: anxiety causes hyperventilation which INCREASES SpO2, not decreases it. The difference is the vital signs.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of anxiety features. What made PE unlikely here?
feedback.incorrect: Anxiety IS a real diagnosis - but only after excluding organic causes. Normal SpO2 (99%) + negative D-dimer + low Wells = PE ruled out. NOW can consider anxiety. The key distinguishing feature: anxiety → hyperventilation → HIGH SpO2. Low SpO2 = not anxiety.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Low SpO2 + risk factors = cannot be anxiety alone. Normal SpO2 + negative D-dimer + low Wells = anxiety is appropriate diagnosis. The vital signs tell you which path to take.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient became hypotensive?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same PE but now with BP 78/50, near-syncope, RV failure

chiefComplaint.original: I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient with confirmed PE now BP 78/50, nearly passed out

shouldRise: Need for systemic thrombolytics or thrombectomy

feedback.correct: Unstable massive PE = consider SYSTEMIC THROMBOLYTICS (tPA) or catheter-directed therapy. Anticoagulation alone is insufficient. High mortality without aggressive intervention. Benefits outweigh bleeding risks.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of instability. What treatment escalation is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Massive PE with hemodynamic instability: anticoagulation alone is INSUFFICIENT. Options: systemic tPA (fastest), catheter-directed thrombolysis, surgical embolectomy. Decision based on availability and contraindications. Don't delay - mortality is high.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Submassive PE (RV strain but stable) = anticoagulation, consider lytics. Massive PE (hypotensive) = systemic thrombolytics or thrombectomy. Instability changes the risk-benefit calculation.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a recent hemorrhagic stroke?
whatIfScenarios.description: Massive PE but had hemorrhagic stroke 2 weeks ago - lytics contraindicated

chiefComplaint.original: I feel like I can't breathe and my heart is racing
chiefComplaint.modified: Massive PE, hypotensive, but had hemorrhagic stroke 2 weeks ago

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Massive PE with contraindication to systemic thrombolytics

feedback.correct: Recent hemorrhagic stroke = ABSOLUTE contraindication to systemic tPA. Options: catheter-directed thrombolysis (lower bleeding risk), surgical embolectomy, or IVC filter if all else contraindicated. Don't give up - there are alternatives.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of contraindication. What are the alternative options?
feedback.incorrect: When systemic thrombolytics are contraindicated (recent hemorrhagic stroke, recent surgery, active bleeding): consider catheter-directed thrombolysis (more localized, lower systemic bleeding), surgical thrombectomy, or mechanical thrombectomy. IVC filter if can't anticoagulate.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: No contraindications = systemic tPA is fastest option. Contraindications present = catheter-directed or surgical approach. Recent CNS bleed is absolute contraindication to systemic lytics.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.text: Availability bias
options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.

options.text: Anchoring bias
options.explanation: Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.

options.text: Premature closure
options.explanation: Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.

options.text: Confirmation bias
options.explanation: Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

female-62-mi.name: B. Thompson
female-62-mi.chiefComplaint: Bad heartburn that won't go away
female-62-mi.variantDescription: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Looks like GERD, actually inferior STEMI

missedConsequences.Acute Myocardial Infarction: Patient sent home with omeprazole for 'GERD.' Found dead at home 8 hours later. Autopsy reveals acute inferior MI with complete RCA occlusion.
missedConsequences.GERD: If this were actually GERD (it's not), sending home with a PPI would be appropriate. This case demonstrates why you can't assume GI cause of epigastric symptoms in high-risk patients.

commonPitfalls: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the 'obvious' diagnosis (GERD) is WRONG
Women present atypically with ACS more often than men - 'heartburn' is a common presentation
Being fooled by relief with antacids - nitroglycerin relief is also nonspecific, and so is antacid relief
Missing bradycardia - HR 58 in a patient with 'heartburn' should raise suspicion for inferior MI
Not considering ACS because there's no 'crushing chest pain' - atypical presentations are common, especially in women, diabetics, and elderly
Attributing nausea/vomiting to 'stomach flu' - N/V is common with inferior MI (vagal response)

mcqs.question: The patient describes 'heartburn' and some nausea. She took Tums and it helped a little. Her heart rate is 58. What should concern you about this presentation?

options.text: Nothing - this is classic GERD with antacid response
options.explanation: DANGEROUS! Multiple red flags here: bradycardia, nausea, and the patient's age/risk factors.

options.text: Bradycardia + nausea + epigastric symptoms = consider inferior MI
options.explanation: Inferior MI often presents with 'GI' symptoms (nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain) because the inferior wall is near the diaphragm. Bradycardia occurs due to RCA supplying the SA and AV nodes. This combination should trigger ACS workup.

options.text: The bradycardia is probably from beta blocker use
options.explanation: Never assume - and even if she's on a beta blocker, the combination of symptoms still warrants cardiac evaluation.

options.text: Antacid relief rules out cardiac cause
options.explanation: WRONG! Partial relief with antacids does not rule out cardiac cause. Some MI patients report improvement with antacids.

mcqs.question: Women with ACS often present differently than men. Which statement about sex differences in ACS presentation is TRUE?

options.text: Women always have typical crushing chest pain just like men
options.explanation: False. Women are more likely to present with atypical symptoms.

options.text: Women are more likely to present with fatigue, nausea, back pain, or 'indigestion'
options.explanation: Women more often present with: fatigue, dyspnea, nausea, back or jaw pain, and 'indigestion.' They're less likely to report classic crushing substernal chest pain. This contributes to delayed diagnosis and worse outcomes in women with ACS.

options.text: ACS is rare in women so we don't need to worry as much
options.explanation: WRONG! Heart disease is the leading cause of death in women. ACS absolutely occurs in women.

options.text: Women with ACS always have normal ECGs
options.explanation: False. Women can have ECG changes with ACS, though they may be more subtle.

allDiagnoses.name: GERD
allDiagnoses.rationale: 62-year-old with 'heartburn,' history of reflux, some relief with antacids. Seems like GERD but THIS IS A TRAP.

allDiagnoses.name: Peptic Ulcer Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Epigastric pain, nausea. Takes NSAIDs occasionally. Could fit PUD.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ THE ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. 62yo woman with DM, HTN, smoking history. Atypical presentation with epigastric pain, nausea, bradycardia. Inferior MI often mimics GI symptoms!

allDiagnoses.name: Unstable Angina
allDiagnoses.rationale: Even if ECG/troponin initially normal, ACS must be ruled out in this risk profile.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortic Dissection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hypertensive patient. Could present with epigastric pain. Check for tearing quality, BP asymmetry.

allDiagnoses.name: Biliary Colic
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause epigastric pain. But no relation to fatty meals, and bradycardia is unexplained.

allDiagnoses.name: Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Epigastric pain with nausea could fit. But would expect more severe pain, elevated lipase.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: NSAID use could cause gastritis. But bradycardia and risk factors demand cardiac workup.

female-62-mi.oldcartsNarrative: "I've had this heartburn since this morning - maybe 6 hours now. It's like a burning feeling in my upper stomach and chest. I've had reflux before and this feels similar, maybe a little worse. I took some Tums and it helped a little but it came back. I also feel kind of nauseous and threw up once. I'm a little sweaty but I thought that was from being nauseated. No real chest 'pain' - it's more like indigestion. I ate some spicy food last night so I thought maybe that's what triggered it. I do have diabetes and high blood pressure. I smoked for 30 years but quit 5 years ago. My mom died of a heart attack at 68. I've been under a lot of stress lately."

historyQuestions.text: When exactly did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: This morning around 6 AM. So it's been about 6 hours now. I woke up with it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 6 hours of continuous symptoms is concerning. GERD typically comes and goes. Prolonged 'indigestion' in high-risk patient = ACS until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Describe exactly what you're feeling.
historyQuestions.answer: It's a burning feeling, kind of tight too, in my upper stomach and lower chest. It's hard to describe. It's not sharp, more like pressure and burning mixed together.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Burning AND pressure/tightness should raise concern for cardiac etiology. Classic GERD is typically just burning without pressure sensation.

historyQuestions.text: Does the discomfort go anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: Now that you mention it, my jaw has been achy and I thought I slept funny. And my left arm feels a little heavy or tired.
historyQuestions.reasoning: JAW AND ARM SYMPTOMS - classic ACS radiation! Patient didn't volunteer this because she didn't connect it to her 'heartburn.' Always ask specifically.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the nausea and vomiting.
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt queasy all morning and threw up once. I thought maybe I had a stomach bug or food poisoning from the spicy food.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting is common with INFERIOR MI due to vagal activation. It's also why patients often attribute symptoms to 'stomach' issues.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been sweating?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I'm kind of clammy. I thought it was from being nauseous.
historyQuestions.reasoning: DIAPHORESIS - a red flag! Cold sweats with 'GI' symptoms should always trigger concern for ACS. Patients often attribute it to nausea.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your medical history.
historyQuestions.answer: I have diabetes - been on metformin for 10 years. High blood pressure too, on lisinopril. I smoked for 30 years and quit 5 years ago. My mom died of a heart attack when she was 68.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MAJOR CARDIAC RISK FACTORS: DM, HTN, 30 pack-year smoking history, family history of premature CAD. This patient is HIGH RISK for ACS.

historyQuestions.text: You said Tums helped - tell me about that.
historyQuestions.answer: I took a few Tums and it seemed to get a little better for maybe 20 minutes, then came right back. I've gone through half a bottle today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Partial, transient relief with antacids does NOT rule out ACS. Response is nonspecific. The key is that symptoms keep returning.

historyQuestions.text: Does activity make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been lying on the couch all day so I haven't really moved around much. I did get more uncomfortable when I walked to the bathroom.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Worsening with exertion supports cardiac etiology.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Is this related to eating? Does it get worse after meals?
historyQuestions.answer: I thought it was at first - it seemed to come on after I ate lunch. But honestly it's been pretty constant for the past few hours regardless of whether I eat or not.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ⚠️ TRAP: Initial postprandial timing may mislead toward GERD, but CONSTANT symptoms regardless of eating argues AGAINST typical reflux. True GERD fluctuates with meals and position. Continuous symptoms should raise concern for cardiac etiology.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_stress_recent.answer: My husband's been in the hospital. I've been running back and forth, not sleeping well, eating whatever I can grab.
social_stress_recent.relevance: Acute stress is recognized trigger for ACS. Poor sleep and diet compound cardiovascular risk.

occupation_past_current.answer: I'm retired now. Used to work in accounting. Desk job for 30 years.
occupation_past_current.relevance: Sedentary occupation history contributes to long-term cardiovascular risk.

exercise_activity_level.answer: I don't exercise much. My knees bother me. I mostly sit around.
exercise_activity_level.relevance: Physical inactivity is major modifiable cardiovascular risk factor.

diet_eating_patterns.answer: With everything going on I've been eating a lot of takeout. More salt and fat than usual.
diet_eating_patterns.relevance: Poor diet during stress period may exacerbate hypertension and cardiovascular risk.

social_medication_adherence.answer: I've been so distracted I might have missed some of my pills this week. It's hard to keep track.
social_medication_adherence.relevance: Medication non-adherence during stressful period - missing BP/cardiac meds increases ACS risk.

housing_living_situation.answer: Home alone while my husband's hospitalized. The kids live far away.
housing_living_situation.relevance: Social isolation and caregiver stress are cardiovascular risk factors.

social_symptom_attribution.answer: I figured it was just stress and bad eating catching up to me. Didn't think it could be my heart.
social_symptom_attribution.relevance: Classic patient reasoning - women often attribute cardiac symptoms to GI causes. Key teaching point about atypical presentations.

social_tobacco_alcohol.answer: Never smoked. Maybe a glass of wine on holidays.
social_tobacco_alcohol.relevance: Non-smoker is protective but doesn't eliminate ACS risk given other factors.

physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 BRADYCARDIA in patient with 'GERD' is a red flag! Inferior MI affects the RCA which supplies the SA and AV nodes, causing bradycardia. Low-normal BP in hypertensive patient is also concerning.

physicalExam.finding: 62-year-old woman appearing uncomfortable, mildly diaphoretic. Clutching her epigastrium. Pallor noted.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diaphoresis and pallor in a 'GI' patient should trigger immediate concern for ACS.

physicalExam.finding: Bradycardic, regular rhythm. Normal S1/S2. Soft S4 gallop. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: S4 gallop indicates decreased ventricular compliance - consistent with acute ischemia causing stiff ventricle.

physicalExam.finding: JVP elevated at approximately 10 cm H2O.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated JVP can indicate right ventricular involvement in inferior MI (RV infarct).

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Clear lungs doesn't rule out MI. In fact, isolated inferior MI often has clear lungs (no LV failure).

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness, non-distended. No guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Benign abdominal exam doesn't rule out MI. Epigastric tenderness can occur with inferior MI due to diaphragmatic irritation.

labs.name: 12-lead ECG
labs.result: ⚠️ ST ELEVATION in leads II, III, aVF (inferior leads). Reciprocal ST depression in I, aVL. ST elevation in V4R (right-sided leads) indicating RV involvement.
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: This is an INFERIOR STEMI with RV involvement. Requires emergent cardiac catheterization.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Markedly elevated troponin confirms myocardial necrosis. Don't wait for this result to activate cath lab in STEMI.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis can be seen with MI (stress response).

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 1.0, Glucose 245 (elevated - stress hyperglycemia in diabetic).
labs.reasoning: Hyperglycemia common in MI due to stress response. Important for prognosis.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No pulmonary edema. No cardiomegaly. Clear lungs.
imaging.reasoning: Normal CXR doesn't rule out MI. In inferior MI without LV failure, CXR is often normal.

treatments.name: Aspirin 325mg
treatments.reasoning: IMMEDIATE - aspirin reduces mortality in ACS. Give as soon as ACS suspected.

treatments.name: Anticoagulation (heparin)
treatments.result: Heparin bolus and infusion started per ACS protocol.

treatments.name: Nitroglycerin
treatments.result: HOLD - patient has RV involvement (seen on right-sided leads). Nitrates contraindicated!
treatments.reasoning: ⚠️ CAUTION: In RV infarct, nitrates can cause severe hypotension. RV depends on preload; nitrates reduce preload. Give fluids instead.

treatments.name: Omeprazole for GERD
treatments.result: NOT APPROPRIATE - this is not GERD, this is STEMI!
treatments.reasoning: Treating this as GERD would be fatal. This is a STEMI requiring emergent reperfusion.

treatments.name: IV fluids (cautious bolus)
treatments.result: 500mL NS bolus given - BP improved to 118/72.
treatments.reasoning: In RV infarct, the RV is preload-dependent. Fluids improve RV output. Opposite of LV failure treatment.

consults.name: Activate cardiac cath lab
consults.result: STEMI alert activated. Cardiology and cath lab team mobilized. Door-to-balloon time goal <90 minutes.
consults.reasoning: STEMI requires emergent PCI. Time is myocardium - every minute of delay = more heart damage.

consults.name: GI consult for GERD
consults.reasoning: This is not a GI problem. GI consult would delay life-saving cardiac care.

disposition.name: Emergent cardiac catheterization
disposition.result: Patient taken to cath lab. 100% RCA occlusion found and stented. Good flow restored. Patient stable in CCU.
disposition.reasoning: STEMI = emergent PCI. Door-to-balloon time directly correlates with outcomes.

disposition.name: Discharge home with PPI and GI follow-up
disposition.result: FATAL CHOICE - patient would die of MI at home
disposition.reasoning: Sending this patient home with GERD treatment would result in death.

actualDiagnosis.name: Inferior STEMI with RV Involvement
keyFindings: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Presented as 'heartburn' but is life-threatening STEMI
Bradycardia (HR 58) - RCA supplies SA/AV nodes; inferior MI causes bradycardia
Nausea/vomiting - common with inferior MI due to vagal activation
Diaphoresis - 'cold sweats' with GI symptoms = cardiac until proven otherwise
Risk factors: DM, HTN, 30 pack-year smoking, family history of MI
Atypical symptoms in woman - 'indigestion' without classic chest pain
ECG: ST elevation II, III, aVF with reciprocal changes and RV involvement
RV infarct: Avoid nitrates (preload-dependent), give fluids instead
teachingPoints: 🚨 WOMEN PRESENT ATYPICALLY: Women with ACS more often present with fatigue, nausea, 'indigestion,' back/jaw pain rather than classic crushing chest pain. Maintain high suspicion!
INFERIOR MI = GI SYMPTOMS: The inferior wall is near the diaphragm. Inferior MI commonly causes nausea, vomiting, and epigastric discomfort that mimics GI disease.
BRADYCARDIA = THINK INFERIOR MI: The RCA supplies the SA and AV nodes. Inferior MI often presents with bradycardia or heart block. HR 58 with 'heartburn' is a red flag!
ANTACID RELIEF IS NONSPECIFIC: Partial improvement with antacids does not rule out ACS. Don't be fooled by this.
RV INFARCT MANAGEMENT IS DIFFERENT: RV depends on preload. Avoid nitrates and diuretics (reduce preload). Give fluids to maintain RV filling.
ALWAYS CHECK RIGHT-SIDED LEADS: In inferior STEMI, get right-sided ECG (V4R) to assess for RV involvement. Changes management.
TIME IS MYOCARDIUM: STEMI requires emergent reperfusion. Every 30-minute delay in door-to-balloon time increases mortality.

closureScript.diagnosis: Mrs. Thompson, I have important news. What you've been experiencing isn't heartburn - it's a heart attack. I know that's surprising because you didn't have the typical 'crushing chest pain' people hear about. But women often experience heart attacks differently, with symptoms that feel more like indigestion or nausea. Your heart tracing and blood tests confirm this.
closureScript.plan: We're taking you to the cardiac catheterization lab right now - this is our top priority. There's a blocked artery supplying blood to your heart, and we need to open it as quickly as possible. The cardiologist will put a small tube in through your wrist or leg, find the blockage, and place a small metal tube called a stent to keep it open. Every minute counts, so we're moving fast.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Right now, just try to stay calm and let us do our job. The team is excellent and this is what we do every day. We've given you medications to help your heart and thin your blood.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is terrifying and completely unexpected. You came in thinking you had heartburn, and now we're rushing you to surgery. But you did the right thing by coming in, and we caught this in time. Many women don't come to the hospital with these symptoms because they don't realize it could be their heart. You may have saved your own life.

guidelineReferences.name: ACC/AHA STEMI Guidelines 2013
keyPoints: Door-to-balloon time <90 minutes for primary PCI
Women present atypically more often than men
Right-sided ECG for inferior STEMI to assess RV
Avoid nitrates in RV infarction (preload-dependent)
Immediate reperfusion strategy is priority

guidelineReferences.name: AHA Scientific Statement: ACS in Women 2016
keyPoints: Women have higher mortality from ACS than men
Atypical presentations more common in women
GI symptoms may mask cardiac disease
Risk factor modification equally important
Sex-specific differences in outcomes

whatIfScenarios.title: What if ECG and troponin were normal?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same epigastric burning but normal ECG, negative troponin, typical GERD features

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this burning in my chest and stomach for two days
chiefComplaint.modified: Same burning, but ECG normal, troponin negative, no risk factors, relieved completely by antacids

mustNotMiss: Still maintain some suspicion for ACS

feedback.correct: Normal ECG + negative troponin + typical GERD features (postprandial, lying down, antacid relief) = GERD is reasonable diagnosis. Can trial PPI. BUT if any cardiac risk factors, consider serial troponins and stress testing as outpatient.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of GERD features. When can you be confident it's NOT cardiac?
feedback.incorrect: GERD IS a real diagnosis when cardiac is truly ruled out: normal ECG, negative troponins x2, low risk factors. Features of GERD: postprandial, worse lying down, sour taste, COMPLETE relief with antacids (partial relief doesn't count).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: ACS = abnormal ECG and/or troponin, risk factors, incomplete antacid relief. True GERD = normal cardiac workup, typical features, complete antacid relief. The workup, not just symptoms, makes the diagnosis.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient developed complete heart block?
whatIfScenarios.description: Inferior STEMI with sudden HR drop to 35, near-syncope

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this burning in my chest and stomach for two days
chiefComplaint.modified: Same patient with inferior STEMI, now HR 35, nearly passed out

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Complete heart block complicating inferior MI

feedback.correct: Inferior MI commonly causes heart block (RCA supplies AV node). If unstable: atropine (may work in inferior MI), transcutaneous pacing, then transvenous. Usually resolves after reperfusion. Still need emergent cath!
feedback.partial: Good recognition of heart block in inferior MI. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Inferior MI + complete heart block: RCA supplies AV node, so inferior MI commonly causes bradycardia/block. Treatment: atropine (often works in inferior MI unlike anterior), pacing if needed. Usually transient, resolves after reperfusion. Don't delay cath for pacing.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Inferior MI → AV nodal dysfunction → usually responsive to atropine, often transient. Anterior MI → infranodal block → usually NOT responsive to atropine, often requires permanent pacing. Location of MI determines behavior.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if blood pressure dropped with nitroglycerin?
whatIfScenarios.description: Gave sublingual nitro for chest pain, BP crashed from 110/70 to 70/40

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this burning in my chest and stomach for two days
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient with inferior STEMI given sublingual nitro, BP now 70/40

feedback.correct: RV infarct is PRELOAD-DEPENDENT. Nitrates reduce preload → RV can't fill → hypotension. Treatment: STOP nitrates, give IV FLUIDS (counterintuitive but correct), avoid diuretics. Always check V4R for RV involvement in inferior STEMI.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of RV infarct. Why did nitro cause this?
feedback.incorrect: RV infarct (common with inferior MI): RV becomes stiff, depends on preload to fill. Nitrates reduce preload → RV empties → cardiac output drops. Treatment: IV fluids (even if JVP elevated!), AVOID nitrates and diuretics. Get V4R to confirm RV involvement.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: LV failure = elevated preload bad, nitrates helpful, give diuretics. RV failure = preload-dependent, nitrates dangerous, give fluids. Completely opposite management. Always check for RV involvement in inferior STEMI.

male-68-aaa.name: R. Franklin
male-68-aaa.chiefComplaint: My back has been killing me since this morning
male-68-aaa.variantDescription: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Looks like musculoskeletal back pain, actually ruptured AAA

missedConsequences.Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (Ruptured): Patient given muscle relaxants for 'back sprain' and sent home. Collapses in parking lot. Dies before returning to ED. Autopsy reveals 8cm ruptured infrarenal AAA.
missedConsequences.Musculoskeletal Back Pain: If this were actually MSK back pain (it's not), conservative treatment would be appropriate. This case shows why vital signs matter and why older patients with new back pain need abdominal evaluation.

commonPitfalls: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the 'obvious' diagnosis (back strain) is WRONG
Not recognizing HYPOTENSION + TACHYCARDIA as signs of shock in patient with 'back pain'
Assuming back pain in older man is musculoskeletal without examining abdomen
Missing the pulsatile abdominal mass on exam (only found if you LOOK for it)
Not considering AAA in any patient >50 with new back, flank, or abdominal pain
Attributing syncope to 'vasovagal' in patient with hypotension
Getting CT scan before calling surgery - delays life-saving intervention

mcqs.question: A 68-year-old male smoker presents with back pain and near-syncope. His BP is 92/58, HR 112. What should this vital sign combination immediately trigger?

options.text: Concern for dehydration - give him fluids and reassess
options.explanation: While fluids may be needed, this is SHOCK until proven otherwise in an older man with back pain.

options.text: These vitals are consistent with pain - give pain medication first
options.explanation: DANGEROUS! Pain alone doesn't cause hypotension. This is shock.

options.text: High suspicion for ruptured AAA - this is shock until proven otherwise
options.explanation: Hypotension + tachycardia + syncope + back pain in older male = AAA until proven otherwise. This is a surgical emergency. Call vascular surgery BEFORE imaging.

options.text: Orthostatic hypotension from getting up too fast
options.explanation: Orthostatic changes don't typically cause this degree of hypotension in supine position.

mcqs.question: You palpate a pulsatile mass in the abdomen. The patient is hypotensive. What is the next step?

options.text: Get CT angiogram to confirm diagnosis before calling surgery
options.explanation: NO! In unstable patient with suspected ruptured AAA, CT delays surgery. Call surgery first.

options.text: Call vascular surgery IMMEDIATELY for emergent OR
options.explanation: CORRECT. Hemodynamically unstable patient with pulsatile mass = ruptured AAA. Call surgery NOW. CT only if patient is stable and diagnosis uncertain.

options.text: Get bedside ultrasound to measure the aneurysm size
options.explanation: Bedside US can confirm AAA presence but delays definitive care in unstable patient.

options.text: Start IV fluids and observe - surgery can wait for morning
options.explanation: FATAL error. Ruptured AAA mortality is 80% without surgery. Every minute counts.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Back Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: 68-year-old with back pain, reports no specific injury but was 'moving furniture.' Could be strain. BUT the vital signs don't fit!

allDiagnoses.name: Lumbar Radiculopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Could explain back pain. But doesn't explain hypotension, tachycardia, or syncope.

allDiagnoses.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (Ruptured)
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ THE ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. 68yo male, smoker, HTN history. Hypotension + tachycardia + syncope + back pain = AAA until proven otherwise!

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Myocardial Infarction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with back pain. Must rule out with ECG and troponin.

allDiagnoses.rationale: Tearing back pain, hypertension history. Would expect severe, tearing quality.

allDiagnoses.name: Pancreatitis

male-68-aaa.oldcartsNarrative: "My back started hurting this morning, right in the middle, kind of towards my lower back. It came on pretty suddenly while I was helping my son move some boxes. I thought I just pulled something, but the pain has been getting worse all day. It's a deep, aching pain. Earlier I felt really lightheaded when I stood up and almost passed out - had to grab the counter. I've been sweating and feel kind of weak. I took some ibuprofen but it didn't help. I've smoked for 40 years. I have high blood pressure but I'm not great about taking my pills. No, I've never had anything like this before. My father died of something with his belly when he was about my age - I don't remember what it was."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: This morning, maybe 6-7 hours ago. I was helping my son move some boxes and the pain came on pretty suddenly. I thought I just pulled something but it's been getting worse all day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset of back pain that worsens over hours is concerning for AAA leak rather than simple musculoskeletal strain

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about almost passing out.
historyQuestions.answer: I stood up to go to the bathroom and everything went gray. I grabbed the counter and sat down quick. I was sweating and felt like I might throw up. It took a few minutes to feel okay again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Syncope with back pain = AAA rupture until proven otherwise. The 'pre-syncope' represents hemorrhagic shock.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain - is it sharp, dull, tearing?
historyQuestions.answer: It's a deep ache. Steady, not coming and going. It's mostly in my back but I feel it in my belly too, kind of a full feeling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Back AND abdominal pain together suggests retroperitoneal process. AAA rupture often presents with back > abdominal pain.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your smoking history.
historyQuestions.answer: I've smoked a pack a day for about 40 years. I know I should quit but I haven't been able to.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 40 pack-year smoking history is MAJOR risk factor for AAA. Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned your father - what happened to him?
historyQuestions.answer: He died suddenly when he was about 70. Something burst in his belly, they said. It happened so fast.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of AAA (likely what killed his father) significantly increases risk. First-degree relatives have 2x risk.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your blood pressure.
historyQuestions.answer: They tell me it's high. I have pills but I forget to take them a lot. Last time it was checked it was something like 160 over something.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic hypertension is major risk factor for both AAA and aortic dissection.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any imaging of your abdomen - ultrasound, CT scan?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. I haven't been to a doctor much.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Many AAAs are found incidentally on imaging. This patient has never been screened despite risk factors.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_tobacco_history.answer: Smoked for about 40 years. A pack a day, sometimes more. Quit maybe 10 years ago.
social_tobacco_history.relevance: 40 pack-year smoking history is single greatest risk factor for AAA. Even after quitting, AAA risk persists.

occupation_past_physical_labor.answer: Construction worker my whole life. Heavy lifting, hard work. That's why my back's bad.
occupation_past_physical_labor.relevance: Physical labor history creates expectation of back pain - classic reason AAA is missed ('must be my bad back').

social_healthcare_access.answer: Don't have insurance. Haven't seen a doctor in years. Can't afford it.
social_healthcare_access.relevance: No AAA screening despite high-risk profile (male, smoker, hypertensive, >65). Screening would have caught this.

social_medication_adherence.answer: Got blood pressure pills but they're expensive. I don't take them like I should.
social_medication_adherence.relevance: Uncontrolled hypertension accelerates AAA growth and rupture risk.

diet_eating_habits.answer: Fast food mostly. Whatever's cheap and easy. Don't have anyone cooking for me.
diet_eating_habits.relevance: Poor diet contributes to hypertension and cardiovascular disease burden.

housing_living_situation.answer: Live alone. Divorced. Kids don't come around much.
housing_living_situation.relevance: Social isolation affects healthcare utilization and health behaviors.

social_alcohol_use.answer: Few beers most days. More on weekends. Helps with the back pain.
social_alcohol_use.relevance: Regular alcohol use may mask symptoms and contribute to overall cardiovascular risk.

occupation_barriers_to_care.answer: When I was working, couldn't take time off to see doctors. Now I just don't have the money.
occupation_barriers_to_care.relevance: Occupational and financial barriers delayed diagnosis of treatable condition that's now emergent.

physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 SHOCK! Hypotension + tachycardia = hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise. Do NOT attribute to pain!

physicalExam.finding: 68-year-old male appearing pale, diaphoretic, and uncomfortable. Lying still, doesn't want to move.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor and diaphoresis indicate poor perfusion. Classic shock appearance.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen soft but mildly distended. PULSATILE MASS palpable in midline/periumbilical region, approximately 6-8 cm. Diffuse mild tenderness. No peritoneal signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 PULSATILE ABDOMINAL MASS = AAA! Combined with hypotension, this is DIAGNOSTIC of ruptured AAA. Call surgery NOW.

physicalExam.finding: Mild tenderness diffusely over lumbar spine. No step-off. No costovertebral angle tenderness. No obvious deformity.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-specific back findings. The back pain in AAA is referred from the retroperitoneal hemorrhage.

physicalExam.finding: Femoral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Dorsalis pedis pulses weak but palpable. Delayed capillary refill (4 seconds).
physicalExam.reasoning: Delayed capillary refill indicates poor perfusion. Femoral pulses present but patient is hypotensive.

physicalExam.finding: Normal tone. No masses. Stool brown, guaiac negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out GI bleeding as cause of shock.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 14.2, Hgb 9.8 (LOW - was 14.2 three months ago), Plt 198.
labs.reasoning: Hemoglobin drop indicates blood loss. Compare to prior values if available.

labs.name: Type and Screen / Crossmatch
labs.result: Type O positive. 6 units PRBCs crossmatched and available.
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL - patient will need massive transfusion. Send this immediately.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion. Hyperkalemia can occur with tissue ischemia.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate confirms shock physiology. Tissue hypoxia from hemorrhage.

labs.name: ECG
labs.result: Sinus tachycardia at 112. No ST changes. No evidence of acute ischemia.
labs.reasoning: Rules out MI as cause of presentation. Tachycardia is compensatory for blood loss.

imaging.name: Bedside Ultrasound (FAST + Aorta)
imaging.result: Large infrarenal AAA measuring approximately 7.5 cm. Free fluid in retroperitoneum. Unable to determine if actively leaking.
imaging.reasoning: Bedside US can rapidly confirm AAA. Definitive imaging only if patient stable.

imaging.name: CT Angiogram of Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: DO NOT DELAY SURGERY FOR THIS - only if patient stabilizes.
imaging.reasoning: In unstable patient, CT delays surgery. Go to OR based on clinical diagnosis.

imaging.name: Lumbar spine X-ray
imaging.result: NOT APPROPRIATE - misses the diagnosis and delays care.
imaging.reasoning: X-ray won't show AAA and delays life-saving treatment.

treatments.name: Large-bore IV access + Fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Two 18g IVs placed. 2L crystalloid hung, running wide open. Pressure increased to 98/62.
treatments.reasoning: Resuscitation while preparing for OR. Goal is permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) to avoid disrupting clot.

treatments.name: Packed red blood cells
treatments.result: 2 units O-negative given emergently. Type-specific blood on the way.
treatments.reasoning: Patient needs blood replacement. Don't wait for crossmatch in exsanguinating patient.

treatments.name: Pain medication (IV morphine)
treatments.reasoning: Small doses for comfort. Be cautious as opioids can worsen hypotension.

treatments.name: Muscle relaxant for 'back sprain'
treatments.reasoning: Missing the diagnosis. Would send patient home to die.

consults.name: EMERGENT Vascular Surgery
consults.result: Vascular surgery at bedside within 5 minutes. OR being prepared. Patient to OR within 20 minutes of arrival.
consults.reasoning: LIFE-SAVING. Ruptured AAA mortality is 80-90% without surgery. Call BEFORE imaging.

consults.name: Orthopedics for back pain
consults.reasoning: This is not a musculoskeletal problem.

disposition.name: Emergent OR for open AAA repair
disposition.result: Patient taken emergently to OR. Ruptured 7.5cm infrarenal AAA found. Successful open repair with tube graft. Patient survived.
disposition.reasoning: Only definitive treatment. Door-to-OR time directly correlates with survival.

disposition.name: Discharge with NSAIDs for back pain
disposition.reasoning: Fatal error. Patient would exsanguinate at home.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA)
keyFindings: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Presented as 'back pain' but is vascular catastrophe
SHOCK: Hypotension (92/58) + tachycardia (112) = hemorrhagic shock
Syncope/pre-syncope - from blood loss, NOT vasovagal
PULSATILE ABDOMINAL MASS - diagnostic finding, only found if you examine abdomen
Risk factors: 68yo male, 40 pack-year smoking, HTN, family history
Back pain + abdominal pain = retroperitoneal process
Hemoglobin drop from 14.2 to 9.8 = acute blood loss
teachingPoints: 🚨 AAA TRIAD: Back/flank/abdominal pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension = ruptured AAA. Don't need all three - any combination with risk factors warrants workup.
VITAL SIGNS MATTER: Pain alone does NOT cause hypotension. Hypotension + tachycardia = shock until proven otherwise.
ALWAYS EXAMINE THE ABDOMEN: In older patients with back pain, examine the abdomen! Pulsatile mass is diagnostic.
SMOKING IS KEY RISK FACTOR: AAA screening recommended for men 65-75 with any smoking history. This patient was never screened.
DON'T DELAY FOR CT: In unstable patient with clinical diagnosis, call surgery FIRST. CT only if stable and diagnosis uncertain.
PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION: Goal SBP 80-90 in ruptured AAA. Aggressive fluid resuscitation can disrupt clot and increase bleeding.
TIME IS LIFE: 80-90% mortality without surgery. Every minute to OR matters. Survival is 50% even WITH surgery.

closureScript.diagnosis: Mr. Franklin, I have critical news. Your back pain isn't from lifting boxes - you have a large aneurysm in your aorta, the main blood vessel in your abdomen, and it's leaking. This is a life-threatening emergency. The near-fainting, the low blood pressure, and your pale appearance all tell us you're losing blood internally.
closureScript.plan: We're taking you to surgery right now. The vascular surgeon is on their way. We're giving you blood transfusions and preparing the operating room as we speak. They'll repair the aneurysm and stop the bleeding. I won't lie to you - this is very serious surgery with significant risks. But without it, you won't survive.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: The surgical team will explain the procedure and risks. Right now, we need to move fast. Is there someone we should call for you?
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know you came in thinking you pulled a muscle. I'm sorry this is such shocking news. But you did the right thing coming in, and we caught this while we can still help you. Many people with this condition don't make it to the hospital.

guidelineReferences.name: SVS Practice Guidelines for AAA 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: Chaikof EL et al. J Vasc Surg. 2018;67(1):2-77
keyPoints: Screening: one-time ultrasound for men 65-75 with smoking history
Elective repair threshold: ≥5.5cm men, ≥5.0cm women
Ruptured AAA: emergent surgery without delay for imaging
Symptomatic AAA: urgent repair regardless of size
Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) in rupture

guidelineReferences.name: USPSTF AAA Screening Recommendation 2019
keyPoints: Grade B: Screen men 65-75 who ever smoked
One-time ultrasound screening
Smoking is strongest risk factor
Family history increases risk 2-fold
Screening reduces AAA mortality

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was hemodynamically stable?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same AAA but BP 142/88, no syncope, stable

chiefComplaint.original: My back just started killing me
chiefComplaint.modified: I've had back pain for a week. My doctor sent me for an ultrasound and they found a large aneurysm.

feedback.correct: Stable AAA with diameter >5.5cm (men) or >5.0cm (women) = elective repair recommended. If asymptomatic and <5.5cm, surveillance with serial imaging. Vascular surgery consult, not emergent OR.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of stable AAA. What determines need for repair?
feedback.incorrect: AAA management by size: <4cm = monitor annually. 4-5.5cm = monitor q6 months. >5.5cm = elective repair (open or EVAR). Rupture risk increases exponentially with size. Stable patients get elective repair, not emergent surgery.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Ruptured AAA = emergent surgery, don't wait for CT. Symptomatic AAA (pain but stable) = urgent surgery. Asymptomatic AAA = elective repair if >5.5cm, otherwise surveillance. Stability changes urgency completely.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain radiated to both flanks?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe bilateral flank pain with hematuria, looks like renal colic

chiefComplaint.original: My back just started killing me
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe pain in both my sides, like kidney stones. There's blood in my urine.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Consider AAA despite renal colic presentation

feedback.correct: AAA can mimic renal colic (referred pain to flanks, sometimes hematuria from ureteral involvement). BILATERAL flank pain should raise suspicion. In older patient with risk factors, always consider AAA before assuming stones.
feedback.partial: Good thinking about AAA mimicking renal colic. What features raise suspicion?
feedback.incorrect: AAA can present as 'renal colic' - pain radiates to flanks, can have hematuria. Red flags: BILATERAL symptoms (stones rarely bilateral at same time), age >60, vascular disease history, hypotension. Don't anchor on 'kidney stones' in older patients with flank pain.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: True renal colic = unilateral, colicky, young patient, hematuria. AAA mimicking colic = often bilateral/vague, constant pain, older patient with vascular risk factors. Always examine abdomen for pulsatile mass.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a known 4cm AAA being monitored?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient under surveillance for AAA now presents with acute abdominal/back pain

chiefComplaint.original: My back just started killing me
chiefComplaint.modified: I have a small aneurysm my doctor has been watching. Now I have sudden severe pain.

feedback.correct: NEW symptoms in patient with known AAA = impending rupture until proven otherwise. Even 'small' aneurysms can rupture or expand rapidly. Urgent vascular surgery consult, CT angiogram if stable, emergent surgery if unstable.
feedback.partial: Good concern for symptomatic AAA. What's the significance of new symptoms?
feedback.incorrect: Symptomatic AAA (new pain in patient with known aneurysm) = possible expansion or impending rupture. Don't be reassured by 'small' size on previous imaging. Aneurysms can expand rapidly. New symptoms = urgent evaluation, likely surgery regardless of size.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Asymptomatic AAA <5.5cm = surveillance appropriate. Symptomatic AAA (new pain) = urgent surgery indicated regardless of size. New symptoms change the game.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
